EVENT_NARRATIVE: A frontal zone brought numerous showers to St Croix from Wednesday night through Thursday. Spotter reports from Fredericksted indicated rainfall accumulations of 3.88 inches for the period.
A funnel cloud was reported off the southeast coast of St Croix.

A massive brush fire was reported on the East End. The fire erupted near Tide Village and quickly spread to hillsides surrounding Lowry Hill and Estate Boetzberg. The fire consumed more than 200 acres of hillside and pastureland.

Funnel cloud reported over the south side of St Thomas. There were no reports of funnel cloud reaching water or land as it crossed over the Havensight area. The same dissipated while traveling north over land.

Heavy rains affected St Croix during the evening. Doppler radar estimated around two inches had fallen over parts of St Croix. Wind gusts of around 40 mph were recorded as a thunderstorm affected the island.
A brush fire on the east end of the island consumed more than one 100 acres of parched vegetation near Grape Tree Bay. The fire damaged several utility poles.
A brush fire scorched more than 300 acres of vegetation near South Sore cafe in Estate Petronelli.  several utlity poles were damaged.
More than 15 acres of brush was scorched when a fire crept over an open field between Estates Mon Bijou and Calquohoun.

Brush fires fueled by brisk winds scorched hundred of acres on St. Croix. Fires  were in estates Bethlehem, Calquohoun and Cobble. The fire damaged several telephone poles and some livestock could have perished.
Two brush fires developed on the west  end of St. Croix, in a field next to Williams's Delight. Morev than 40 acres burned.

The lack of rainfall in St Croix led to the second driest March on record.  Only 0.42 inches of rain were recorded at the airport for the entire month where the normal is 1.72 inches. An estimated of 1,000 acres had burned in brush fires.

Thunderstorms moved across the the middle and eastern half of St Croix. A stronger cell was reported moving inland near Salt River. Lightning struck the St. John Episcopal Church in Christiansted and demolished half of the steeple on top of the facade. Three vehicles were damaged by the falling debris.

Showers and thunderstorms affected St Croix Monday night into Tuesday morning causing street and gut flooding. Unofficial rainfall reports indicated accumulations between 2 and 3 inches during a one hour period.

Showers and thunderstorms associated with a strong tropical wave produced minor flooding conditions in St Croix. Localized flooding and street ponding were reported on East End Road, South Shore Road, La Grande Princess, Williams Delight and some sections of Christiansted and Fredericksted.
Mid afternoon heavy rains flooded out roads on the western part of St. Croix. The Williams Delight community was affected by urban flooding and ponding of water on low lying poorly drained areas.

Rain bands and gusty winds from the northern fringes of Tropical Storm Emily affected the territory Wednesday evening. Squall lines with showers and thunderstorms produced gusty winds. An unofficial report from a spotter at Maria Hill reported a 61 mph gust.
Street flooding was reported in Christiansted.

Heavy showers affected the island and flooded roads, forcing the University of the Virgin Islands to cancel classes. The persistent showers flooded roads in Santa Maria, Lower Contant and Frenchtown. The heavy rains also caused problems in Havensight, Mandela Circle, Turpentine Run and Lindbergh bay.

Heavy rains turned roads into rivers and flooded low lying areas of St Croix. Sections of the road were blocked off with large rocks in La Grande Princesse. Other areas that experienced significant flooding were parts of Queen Mary Highway in Estate Carlton, St. Georges, Plessen, Lower Love, La Reine and Sion Farm. Parts of Williams Delight, La Grande Princesse and the main Gallows Bay intersection.
Heavy showers affected the St. Thomas causing floods that shut down schools and businesses.

Stormwater runoff overwhelmed the storm sewers. Heavy periods of rain caused large amount of runoff and debris to flow from Recovery Hill down to King Cross and Prince street and to the watergut area near Seaborne Airlines.
The heavy rains led to minor mudslides on Centerline Road from Cruz Bay to Coral Bay.
